Alright, welcome back to your next lesson on scripting. Here's the deal. In spite of how obvious it is that using scripts are such a massive boom to your business, the average salesperson out there, their reaction when you talk about scripts will go anywhere from like mild revulsion, like anachronism, like, ugh, it's like the worst thing in the world, like they're going to bomb it and die if you make them use a script. So here's my question. When I mention the use of scripts, are you excited? You know, yes, I really love scripts. Number two, say, no, no, I hate scripts. I mean, it's not for me. Or number three, or you're like kind of in the middle, I don't really need them or I don't. Be honest here, of those three things, do you love scripts, do you despise scripts, or are you somewhat ambivalent? Which one is it? Alright, so you hate using scripts fair enough, that's a legual honest. Let me explain to you what I think is probably missing from your perspective here. You're probably saying to yourself that, you know, if I use a script, it's going to make me sound wooden or inauthentic. And let me just say that if that was indeed the case, I would say to you absolutely not. Never use the script. If they sound wooden or robotic and they know you're reading from a script, you're done. You can't close anybody. But here's the deal. When you're using straight line scripts the right way and you've been trained using this system, what you've essentially done is you've mastered the art of reading from a script without sounding like you're reading from a script, and you sound fluid and elegant and it just allows you to kick some serious butt, stay ethical. All seven of those things allow you to line up and it increases your closing rate dramatically. Excellent. So you know exactly what I'm talking about here. Scripts are just the lifeblood of making a first class sales presentation and you probably know from the past when you didn't use scripts, you're simply not as effective. The bottom line is that when you're using straight line scripts correctly, it allows you to essentially read from a script without sounding like you're reading from a script. Because if you did, you were wooden or inauthentic, you would never close. And that's the true beauty of a straight line script. It allows you to essentially have that anchor, that script, but you never sound like you're reading from a script. Okay, fair enough. So you're in the middle here. Well let me say this. My guess is that you're probably somewhat ambivalent here because you've never had the benefit of using a perfectly constructed straight line script. So your fear probably lies in the area that if you use a script, you're going to sound wooden or inauthentic in some way. And by the way, if that was indeed the case, I would say, yeah, you definitely do not want to use a script. But here's the deal. When we're using straight line scripts, what I'm talking about here, the foremost thing is that you've mastered the art of learning how to read a script without sounding like you're reading from a script. And that is the difference. See once you can read a script, but sound perfectly natural, and by the way, unless that script is written perfectly, you can. It's not usable, right? So when we have a straight line script, it's written in such a way that it allows you to use it and sound perfectly natural and it increases your closing rate dramatically. Let me give you a perfect example here. You probably see in the movie Braveheart, right? We all have, right? Great movie, right? That scene when William Wiles played by Mel Gibson, right? He's about to ride up with the blue paint all over his face, right? And he comes out, he's on horseback, the big war horse, right? And there's a thousand or 2,000 Scottish peasants basically, right? They're pitchforks and shields, right? And then the other side, the opposing side, you see the superior English army, right? A couple of thousand Redcoats in perfect formation and so forth, right? And the Scots are looking at this formidable army and they're about to flee, right? So William Wiles comes riding up on us with his blue face and he gives us awesome rousing speeches. Sons of Scotland! Sons of Scotland! I am William Wiles. Tell our enemies that they may take our lives, but they'll never take our freedom! And just like that, the Scots go out and destroy the superior English army and bam, it's a moment in movie history and in real history, right? Now let me ask you a question. Think this through for a second. That awesome speech that Mel Gibson gave. Do you think, oh Mel winged it? I mean, do you think the director said to Mel, hey Mel, listen, we're going to line up a couple thousand extras over here, a couple 3,000 extras over there, the Brits, we'll get a nice Redcoats, right? We're going to wait just till the sun is perfectly setting. I have all these cameras at the perfect angles, like four or five million bucks in the scene and here's what I want you to do. I'm going to paint your face blue and shit, right? And I want you to ride up to this, you know, all the Scots and I want you to just go out there and just motivate them. Just go out there and just get them all riled up. Just imagine what would happen. I mean, what would come out of Mel Gibson's mouth? Who knows? I mean, probably it depends on how much he was drinking. I mean, love Mel Gibson, but hey, Mel's a bit of a wild man, right? So of course they would never do that. You would never risk spending that much money, losing that much time, buying, asking, oh, Mel, to win it. Instead, what they do is they go out and spend five or six million dollars on the world's greatest screenwriter whose job it is to create what? A script, a perfect script that has all the emotion attached, the logic that makes the case, and then you have someone like Mel Gibson, a world-class actor whose job is to master the art of reading the script without sounding like it's a script. He makes it sound perfectly natural. I'll go one better. Every movie you've watched, every TV series that was so emotional to you that you binge watched, you couldn't stop, right? They all had scripts. Every time you were crying or laughing or hated a villain, it was scripted. But you know these unscripted shows? Even those are scripted because they found out in the beginning with reality shows that if they just left it up to the people's devices, it was so boring, 90% of them don't know what to watch. So they started telling them to do this, do that. They scripted out the movements. Every time you've been emotionally engaged, it's because someone who had mastered the art of reading a script without sounding like it got to you. So don't tell me, please just don't cop into that limiting belief that I can't use a script because it will make me sound inauthentic or wouldn't. It's not true. Here's the deal. When we actually get to the next lesson here and we start digging into what really makes a straight line script, a straight line script, you'll see that they're written in a way that allows you to sound absolutely perfect every time with colloquialisms, not like perfect grammar. It shows you how to write them and then practice them and deliver them in a way where you get to sound awesome every single time. And I promise you, when you do that, your closing rate will soar. Now we have to throw a lot of stuff here. So I want to stop here, let you take a quick quiz here, go through it, master this stuff here and then when you're done, I'll meet you in the next lesson in a few minutes. I'll see you there.
